structured.research_question:
Can a paper-based electrochemical biosensor functionalized with anti-CRP
antibodies detect C-reactive protein in whole blood at clinically relevant
concentrations within 10 minutes, without sample preprocessing?
structured.subject: Whole blood samples (CRP analyte)
structured.independent:
Sensor format (paper-based electrochemical biosensor functionalized with
anti-CRP antibodies, vs. laboratory ELISA).
structured.dependent:
Detection sensitivity (limit of detection in mg/L) and time-to-result
(minutes).
structured.conditions:
Whole blood samples spiked with known CRP concentrations spanning 0.1-10
mg/L. No sample preprocessing (no centrifugation, no dilution). Room
temperature operation. Comparison against high-sensitivity ELISA.
structured.expected:
The paper biosensor detects CRP at concentrations below 0.5 mg/L within
10 minutes, matching laboratory ELISA sensitivity.

procedures.intent: To prepare whole blood samples and spike them with known concentrations of C-reactive protein (CRP) to create a standard curve for biosensor and ELISA evaluation.

procedures.name: Paper Biosensor Fabrication and Functionalization
procedures.intent: To fabricate paper-based electrochemical biosensors and functionalize them with anti-CRP antibodies, ready for sample testing.

procedures.name: Electrochemical Biosensor CRP Detection
procedures.intent: To measure CRP concentrations in spiked whole blood samples using the paper-based electrochemical biosensor and record time-to-result.

procedures.name: High-Sensitivity ELISA CRP Detection
procedures.intent: To measure CRP concentrations in spiked whole blood samples using a commercially available high-sensitivity ELISA kit as a gold standard.

procedures.name: Data Analysis and Performance Evaluation
procedures.intent: To calculate detection sensitivity (LOD) and compare time-to-result for both the biosensor and ELISA, establishing the biosensor's performance relative to the hypothesis.

overall_assumptions: Standard laboratory equipment and reagents are available.
Researchers are trained in handling whole blood samples and infectious materials.
Biosafety Level 2 (BSL-2) practices will be followed for handling whole blood.
Commercial ELISA kits are reliable and perform to manufacturer specifications.
Ethical approval for human blood sample collection and use is obtained.
overall_controls: Negative control: Whole blood samples with no spiked CRP.
Positive control: Whole blood samples spiked with a high, known concentration of CRP (e.g., 10 mg/L).
Biosensor blank: Unfunctionalized paper biosensor exposed to whole blood.
ELISA kit controls: Internal positive and negative controls provided with the ELISA kit.

procedures.name: Whole Blood Sample Preparation and Spiking
procedures.intent: To prepare whole blood samples and spike them with known concentrations of C-reactive protein (CRP) to create a standard curve for biosensor and ELISA evaluation.

steps.title: Obtain and Prepare Whole Blood Sample
steps.body_md: Obtain fresh whole blood samples (EDTA or citrate anti-coagulated) from healthy donors. Ensure the blood is at room temperature (20-25°C) before proceeding. Gently invert the blood collection tube 5-10 times to ensure homogeneity, but avoid vigorous shaking that could induce hemolysis.
steps.notes: Using fresh blood is crucial for accurate CRP measurements as CRP levels can change over time in stored samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogenous whole blood sample, no visible clots or hemolysis.
troubleshooting: If visible clots are present: Do not use the sample; obtain a fresh one. Clots can interfere with accurate spiking and biosensor performance.
If heavy hemolysis is observed: Do not use the sample; obtain a fresh one. Hemolysis can artificially elevate CRP readings and interfere with optical detection.

steps.title: Prepare Concentrated CRP Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Carefully weigh out the required mass of lyophilized Human C-Reactive Protein (CRP) to prepare a concentrated stock solution. Dissolve the CRP in 1X PBS (phosphate-buffered saline, pH 7.4) to achieve an initial stock concentration of 100 mg/L. Mix gently by inversion until fully dissolved. For example, to make 1 mL of 100 mg/L stock, weigh 0.1 mg of CRP and dissolve in 1 mL PBS.
steps.notes: Ensure sterile conditions to avoid contamination, as these solutions will be introduced into blood samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, homogenous CRP stock solution.
troubleshooting: If CRP does not dissolve completely: Gently warm to 37°C for 5 minutes and vortex lightly, or extend dissolution time. Avoid vigorous vortexing that can denature the protein.

steps.title: Prepare Intermediate CRP Dilution Series
steps.body_md: From the 100 mg/L CRP stock solution, prepare a series of intermediate CRP dilutions in 1X PBS to minimize pipetting errors when spiking into whole blood. Prepare dilutions at 5 mg/L, 1 mg/L, and 0.2 mg/L. For example, to get 1 mL of 5 mg/L solution, take 50 μL of 100 mg/L stock and add 950 μL of PBS.
steps.notes: These intermediate dilutions should be freshly prepared right before spiking to ensure accuracy.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, accurately diluted CRP solutions.

steps.title: Aliquot Whole Blood for Spiking
steps.body_md: Aliquot specific volumes (e.g., 500 μL) of the homogenous whole blood sample into individual microcentrifuge tubes or appropriate assay tubes for each CRP concentration and for an unspiked control. Ensure accurate and consistent aliquoting.
steps.notes: Use separate tubes for each concentration to avoid cross-contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Consistent aliquots of whole blood in individual tubes.

steps.title: Spike Whole Blood with CRP
steps.body_md: Carefully add appropriate small volumes of the prepared CRP intermediate dilutions or stock solution into the aliquoted whole blood to achieve final target CRP concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 10 mg/L. The spike volume should be ≤ 5% of the total blood volume to minimize dilution effects on the whole blood matrix. For example, to achieve a final concentration of 1 mg/L in 500 μL of blood, add 25 μL of a 20 mg/L CRP solution (which you would prepare from the 100 mg/L stock or by diluting the 5 mg/L solution further). Ensure gentle mixing immediately after spiking by inverting the tube 2-3 times.
todo_for_researcher: Calculate precise spike volumes and prepare specific intermediate dilutions to achieve target final concentrations within the 5% spike volume constraint. Consider preparing an additional intermediate CRP solution, e.g., 20 mg/L, if multiple target concentrations require it to stay within the 5% volumetric spike. For example, to reach 0.1 mg/L, using 5% spike, one would need to spike a 2 mg/L solution. To reach 0.5 mg/L, one would need to spike a 10 mg/L solution etc.
steps.notes: Accurate pipetting of the small spike volumes is critical. If pipetting very small volumes (e.g., <5 μL) directly, consider preparing more dilute intermediate CRP solutions to increase the spike volume and improve accuracy.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Whole blood samples spiked with a gradient of known CRP concentrations.
troubleshooting: If results are not reproducible across replicates: Re-check pipetting technique and calibration. Ensure thorough but gentle mixing after each spike.

steps.title: Incubate Spiked Samples
steps.body_md: Incubate the spiked whole blood samples at room temperature (20-25°C) for a short duration (10-15 minutes) to allow for complete CRP dissolution and equilibrium within the blood matrix. Do not incubate for extended periods to avoid potential changes in blood components or CRP stability.
steps.notes: Maintain consistent temperature across all samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Spiked samples ready for immediate analysis, with CRP uniformly distributed.

success_criteria.how_measured: Preparation of stock and serial dilutions
success_criteria.threshold: Final concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 10 mg/L in whole blood samples.

success_criteria.how_measured: Volume of spike solution added to whole blood
success_criteria.threshold: Spike volume ≤ 5% of the total blood volume for each sample.

success_criteria.how_measured: Visual inspection of whole blood samples
success_criteria.threshold: No visible clots or significant hemolysis in original or spiked blood samples.

procedures.name: Paper Biosensor Fabrication and Functionalization
procedures.intent: To fabricate paper-based electrochemical biosensors and functionalize them with anti-CRP antibodies, ready for sample testing.

steps.title: Prepare Paper Substrate
steps.body_md: Cut chromatography paper (e.g., Whatman Grade 1 CHR) into desired biosensor dimensions (e.g., 1.5 cm x 4 cm rectangles) using a precision cutter. Ensure cuts are clean and free of tearing or fraying.
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact dimensions (length and width) if not 1.5 cm x 4 cm.
steps.notes: The choice of paper impacts capillary flow and mechanical stability. Whatman Grade 1 CHR is a common choice for its uniformity.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Uniformly cut paper rectangles ready for patterning.

steps.title: Pattern Hydrophobic Barriers
steps.body_md: Apply a hydrophobic material (e.g., SU-8 photoresist using photolithography, or wax using a wax printer) to define the electrochemical sensing zones and fluidic channels on the paper substrate. Cure or set the hydrophobic material according to the manufacturer's instructions. Ensure clear, well-defined boundaries to prevent sample wicking outside the desired areas.
equipment_needed: Photolithography equipment (for SU-8) or wax printer (for wax)
Oven or hot plate (if curing is required)
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact hydrophobic patterning method (e.g., wax printing, screen printing, photolithography).
Specify the exact dimensions and layout of the hydrophobic channels/sensing area.
steps.notes: This step is critical for directing fluid flow and electrically isolating different regions of the sensor.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Paper substrates with clearly defined hydrophobic barriers and hydrophilic channels/sensing areas.
troubleshooting: If hydrophobic barriers are indistinct: Re-evaluate pattern design or material application parameters (e.g., wax temperature, SU-8 exposure time).
If sample wicks outside channels: Increase barrier thickness or adjust material properties.

steps.title: Deposit Electrochemical Electrodes
steps.body_md: Deposit conductive material (e.g., carbon ink, silver/silver chloride ink) onto the defined sensing zones using screen printing or stencil printing. Create a three-electrode system: working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode. Cure the conductive ink as per manufacturer's instructions (e.g., oven baking).
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact ink formulations and deposition methods.
Specify electrode dimensions and spacing based on the patterned design.
steps.notes: Careful deposition ensures consistent electrical conductivity and electrochemical performance.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Paper substrates with clearly visible and conductive electrochemical electrodes.
troubleshooting: If electrodes are not conductive: Check printing parameters, ink viscosity, and curing conditions.
If electrodes are smeared: Optimize printing pressure or stencil/screen alignment.

steps.title: Block Non-Specific Binding Sites
steps.body_md: Incubate the fabricated biosensors with a blocking solution (e.g., 1% Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) in Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) or 0.5% Casein in PBS) for 1 hour at room temperature. This step reduces non-specific binding of assay components and analytes to the biosensor surface.
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact blocking agent and its concentration if not 1% BSA or 0.5% Casein.
steps.notes: Adequate blocking is essential for achieving a low background signal and high specificity.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Biosensors visibly wet with blocking solution.
troubleshooting: If high background signal in later steps: Increase blocking agent concentration or incubation time.

steps.title: Wash After Blocking
steps.body_md: Gently rinse the biosensors three times with PBS (pH 7.4) for 5 minutes each wash. Ensure complete removal of unbound blocking solution. Blot excess liquid on a lint-free tissue.
steps.notes: Thorough washing prevents interference with subsequent functionalization steps.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clean biosensor surfaces without visible residue.

steps.title: Immobilize Anti-CRP Antibodies
steps.body_md: Apply 5 µL of anti-CRP antibody solution (100 µg/mL in PBS, pH 7.4) onto the working electrode surface of each biosensor. Incubate in a humid chamber for 2 hours at room temperature to allow for antibody immobilization. Protect from light.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal anti-CRP antibody concentration (100 µg/mL is a starting point, may require optimization).
Specify the exact immobilization chemistry if specific covalent binding is used (e.g., EDC/NHS coupling).
steps.notes: Antibody concentration and immobilization time are critical for achieving high sensitivity and reducing variability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Antibody solution absorbed into the working electrode area.
troubleshooting: If low signal in later steps: Increase antibody concentration or incubation time.
If uneven antibody distribution: Ensure electrode surface is fully hydrated before application.

steps.title: Wash After Antibody Immobilization
steps.body_md: Gently rinse the biosensors three times with PBS containing 0.05% Tween 20 (PBST) for 5 minutes each wash to remove unbound antibodies. Blot excess liquid on a lint-free tissue.
steps.notes: Adequate washing minimizes background noise by removing non-specifically adsorbed antibodies.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clean biosensor surfaces without visible antibody residue.

steps.title: Dry Fabricated Biosensors
steps.body_md: Allow the functionalized biosensors to air dry at room temperature (20-25°C) and controlled humidity (e.g., 40-50% RH) for at least 4 hours, or until completely dry. This can be done in a desiccator or a controlled environment chamber.
steps.notes: Proper drying is crucial for long-term stability and consistent performance. Avoid rapid drying, which can denature antibodies.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Completely dry biosensors, ready for packaging or immediate use.
troubleshooting: If biosensors remain damp: Extend drying time or ensure proper humidity control.
If antibody denatures (loss of function): Reduce drying temperature or consider lyophilization if long-term storage is needed.

steps.title: Package and Store Biosensors
steps.body_md: Package the dried biosensors individually in sealed, opaque, airtight bags containing desiccant packets. Store at 4°C until use. Avoid exposure to light and excessive humidity.
steps.notes: Proper storage extends the shelf life of the functionalized biosensors and maintains antibody stability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Packaged, stable biosensors ready for future experiments.
troubleshooting: If biosensor performance degrades over time: Check storage conditions (temperature, humidity, light exposure) and desiccant efficacy.

success_criteria.threshold: Clear, well-defined boundaries with no delamination or irregular wicking zones.
Resistance < 100 Ohms between working and counter electrodes (example value, needs optimization based on ink/design).

success_criteria.how_measured: Positive signal from a control CRP solution (e.g., 10 mg/L CRP) using cyclic voltammetry or impedance spectroscopy (downstream analytical procedure).
success_criteria.threshold: A measurable electrochemical signal indicating CRP binding, significantly above background.

procedures.name: Electrochemical Biosensor CRP Detection
procedures.intent: To measure CRP concentrations in spiked whole blood samples using the paper-based electrochemical biosensor and record time-to-result.

steps.title: Prepare room temperature environment
steps.body_md: Ensure the laboratory environment is maintained at room temperature (20-25°C). This is crucial for consistent electrochemical measurements and enzymatic reaction kinetics.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Working environment is within the specified temperature range.
troubleshooting: If temperature is outside range: Adjust HVAC or relocate to a controlled environment.

steps.title: Calibrate electrochemical workstation
steps.body_md: Perform a self-calibration and baseline noise check of the potentiostat/galvanostat according to the manufacturer's instructions. Ensure electrodes are clean and connections are secure.
steps.notes: Calibration ensures accurate and reproducible electrochemical readings.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Electrochemical workstation passes self-calibration and displays stable baseline current.
troubleshooting: If calibration fails: Check electrode connections, clean electrodes if necessary, and restart the instrument. Consult manual for error codes.
If high noise is observed: Check for electromagnetic interference, ensure proper grounding.

steps.title: Prepare spiked whole blood samples
steps.body_md: Aliquot 1 mL of each pre-spiked whole blood sample (CRP concentrations spanning 0.1-10 mg/L) into sterile microcentrifuge tubes. Ensure samples are gently inverted 5 times to ensure homogeneity immediately prior to use.
steps.notes: Whole blood can settle; mixing is essential for accurate aliquotting and consistent results.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogeneous 1 mL aliquots of spiked whole blood samples ready for application.

steps.title: Retrieve biosensors and connect to workstation
steps.body_md: Carefully remove a paper-based electrochemical biosensor from its packaging using clean forceps. Connect the biosensor's working, reference, and counter electrodes to the corresponding leads of the electrochemical workstation.
steps.notes: Handle biosensors by the edges to avoid contamination of the active detection area.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Biosensor securely connected to the workstation, ready for sample application.
troubleshooting: If connections are loose: Gently re-seat the electrode leads until secure. Loose connections lead to noisy or erroneous signals.

steps.title: Apply whole blood sample to biosensor
steps.body_md: Pipette a precise volume of 10 µL of the prepared whole blood sample onto the active sensing area of the paper-based biosensor. Immediately start a timer. Avoid introducing air bubbles during application.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the precise 'active sensing area' for sample application based on biosensor design.
steps.notes: Consistent sample volume and precise application location are critical for reproducibility. The timer tracks the incubation and detection time.
steps.anticipated_outcome: 10 µL blood sample evenly spread on the biosensor's active area.
troubleshooting: If air bubbles form: Carefully try to disperse them using the pipette tip without touching the sensing surface, or discard and re-apply with a new sensor if significant.

steps.title: Incubate sample on biosensor
steps.body_md: Allow the whole blood sample to incubate on the biosensor at room temperature for the specified duration (up to 10 minutes). The CRP in the sample will bind to the anti-CRP antibodies immobilized on the sensor surface.
steps.notes: This incubation period allows for sufficient binding kinetics. The specific duration for optimal signal-to-noise ratio within the 10-minute window will be explored.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Binding of CRP to the biosensor surface occurring.

steps.title: Perform electrochemical measurement (Amperometry or Voltammetry)
steps.body_md: At the desired time point (e.g., 5 min, 7 min, or 10 min from sample application), initiate the electrochemical measurement program on the workstation. If using amperometry, apply a constant potential at +0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode and record the current for 60 seconds. If using voltammetry, perform a Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV) scan from -0.2 V to +0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the specific potential and parameters (frequency, amplitude) for amperometry/SWV based on preliminary biosensor characterization. These values are common starting points.
steps.notes: Begin data recording simultaneously with the potential application. The current or peak current will be proportional to the captured CRP concentration after the enzymatic reaction if a reporter enzyme system is used. This step assumes a completed enzymatic reporter step (e.g., alkaline phosphatase/p-aminophenyl phosphate or HRP/TMB) has been performed if needed for signal generation, which might be a preceding step in a full immunoassay. Given the context of a rapid biosensor, it's assumed the signal generation is intrinsic or very rapid.
steps.anticipated_outcome: An electrochemical current (amperometry) or peak current (voltammetry) signal is recorded by the workstation.
troubleshooting: If no signal or noisy signal: Recheck electrode connections, ensure the sample covers the active area, verify workstation settings.

steps.title: Record electrochemical data and time-to-result
steps.body_md: Save the recorded current-time (amperometry) or current-potential (voltammetry) data files. Note the exact time elapsed from sample application (Step 5) to the initiation of the electrochemical measurement (Step 7) as the 'time-to-result'.
steps.notes: Accurate time-to-result recording is a key parameter of this experiment.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Raw electrochemical data and corresponding time-to-result values are saved digitally.
troubleshooting: If data file corruption: Ensure enough disk space and proper software termination. Backup data regularly.

steps.title: Repeat for all samples and controls
steps.body_md: Repeat steps 4 through 8 for all prepared spiked whole blood samples covering the concentration range (0.1-10 mg/L) and any relevant controls (e.g., blank whole blood, unspiked whole blood). Use a fresh biosensor for each measurement.
steps.notes: Using a fresh biosensor for each sample prevents carry-over contamination and ensures independent measurements. A calibration curve will be generated from these measurements.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A complete dataset of electrochemical signals and time-to-result values for all CRP concentrations and controls.

steps.title: Wash biosensor (if applicable)
steps.body_md: If a wash step is integral to the biosensor design (e.g., to remove unbound matrix components), perform a gentle wash immediately following the incubation period (Step 6) and prior to electrochemical measurement (Step 7). Use 100 µL of PBS (pH 7.4) and allow it to wick across the sensor for 30 seconds. Repeat 2 times. Gently blot excess liquid with filter paper, avoiding the active sensing area.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm if a wash step is required for the specific biosensor design provided. If not, omit this step.
steps.notes: A wash step improves signal-to-noise ratio by removing non-specifically bound components and interfering substances from the whole blood matrix.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Reduction in background signal and improved specificity without impacting specifically bound CRP.
troubleshooting: If signal decreases unexpectedly: Excessive or harsh washing might remove bound analytes. Optimize wash volume and duration.
If background remains high: Insufficient washing, increase wash volume or number of repetitions.

success_criteria.how_measured: Coefficient of variation (CV) for replicate measurements at a given CRP concentration

success_criteria.what: Measurable signal across target CRP concentration range
success_criteria.how_measured: Electrochemical signal (current or peak current) increases with increasing CRP concentration
success_criteria.threshold: Clear dose-response curve from 0.1 mg/L to 10 mg/L

success_criteria.how_measured: Recorded time from sample application to signal readout

procedures.name: High-Sensitivity ELISA CRP Detection
procedures.intent: To measure CRP concentrations in spiked whole blood samples using a commercially available high-sensitivity ELISA kit as a gold standard.

steps.title: Prepare Standards and Samples
steps.body_md: Prepare a series of CRP standards using the diluent provided in the high-sensitivity ELISA kit. Create at least 6 standard concentrations spanning the expected linear range of CRP detection (e.g., 0.05 mg/L to 10 mg/L), ensuring at least two points cover the clinically relevant range below 0.5 mg/L.  Prepare the spiked whole blood samples according to the experimental plan (e.g., undiluted whole blood spiked with known CRP concentrations from 0.1-10 mg/L). Allow samples and standards to equilibrate to room temperature (20-25 °C) before use.
equipment_needed: Microcentrifuge tubes or appropriate dilution tubes
steps.notes: Ensure all pipettes are calibrated to ensure accurate dilution of standards.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A complete set of standards and spiked whole blood samples ready for plating.
troubleshooting: If standard curve concentrations are inaccurate: Recalibrate pipettes and redo dilutions.
If reagents are not at room temperature: Allow more time for equilibration to avoid temperature-dependent assay variations.

steps.title: Add Standards and Samples to Microplate
steps.body_md: Remove the microplate from its sealed pouch and allow it to equilibrate to room temperature (20-25 °C). Pipette 50 µL of each standard, control, and spiked whole blood sample into appropriate wells of the ELISA microplate in duplicate or triplicate, as recommended by the kit instructions. Ensure a plate map is meticulously documented.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the exact recommended well volume from the chosen ELISA kit instructions.
steps.notes: Avoid splashing contents between wells. Change pipette tips meticulously between each standard/sample to prevent cross-contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Standards, controls, and samples accurately dispensed into the microplate wells.
troubleshooting: If cross-contamination is suspected: Discard contaminated wells/plate and restart.
If bubbles are present in wells: Gently tap the plate or use a fresh pipette tip to remove them, being careful not to remove liquid.

steps.title: Add Detection Antibody and Incubate
steps.body_md: Add 50 µL of the high-sensitivity CRP detection antibody (conjugate or secondary antibody, as supplied by the kit) to each well containing standards, controls, and samples. Gently tap the plate to ensure thorough mixing. Cover the plate with an adhesive plate seal or lid to prevent evaporation and incubate at room temperature (20-25 °C) for a duration specified in the kit instructions (e.g., 60 minutes).
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit detection antibody
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact incubation time for the chosen high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit.
Confirm if the detection antibody needs prior dilution according to kit instructions.
steps.notes: Ensure even distribution of the detection antibody. Incubation time and temperature are critical for antibody binding kinetics.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Antibodies binding to CRP antigens in the wells.
troubleshooting: If inconsistent signal across plate: Ensure proper mixing and consistent incubation conditions across all wells.
If evaporation: Ensure plate is properly sealed during incubation.

steps.title: Wash Microplate
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the plate seal. Discard the liquid from the wells by inverting the plate over a waste container. Wash each well extensively 3-5 times with 250-300 µL of 1X wash buffer, ensuring no residual liquid remains after each wash by sharply tapping the inverted plate on absorbent paper. Ensure proper aspiration if using an automated plate washer.
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit 1X wash buffer
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the precise number of washes and wash buffer volume specified in the chosen ELISA kit instructions.
steps.notes: Thorough washing is crucial to remove unbound antibody and reduce background signal. Incomplete aspiration leads to high background.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clean wells with minimal background signal, ready for substrate addition.
troubleshooting: If high background signal after washing: Increase number of washes or ensure complete removal of wash buffer.
If wells appear dry or scratched: Adjust plate washer settings or ensure gentle manual washing.

steps.title: Add Substrate and Incubate
steps.body_md: Add 100 µL of the TMB (3,3',5,5'-Tetramethylbenzidine) substrate solution to each well. Gently tap the plate to ensure mixing. Incubate the plate at room temperature (20-25 °C) in the dark for the duration specified in the kit instructions (e.g., 15-30 minutes). Color development will occur during this step, transitioning from clear to blue.
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit TMB substrate solution
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact substrate incubation time for the chosen high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit.
steps.notes: Protect the substrate from light exposure during incubation as it is light-sensitive. Monitor color development, especially for higher concentration standards.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Color development (blue) in wells, proportional to CRP concentration.
troubleshooting: If no color development: Check integrity of substrate solution; ensure proper addition of all reagents.
If color develops too quickly/slowly: Adjust incubation time or check temperature consistency.

steps.title: Add Stop Solution
steps.body_md: Add 50 µL of the stop solution (e.g., sulfuric acid) to each well. Gently tap the plate to ensure thorough mixing. The blue color developed in the previous step will immediately change to yellow, indicating the reaction has been stopped.
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit stop solution
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the exact volume of stop solution required by the chosen ELISA kit.
steps.notes: Add stop solution in the same order and at a similar rate as the substrate to ensure consistent reaction times. Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) as stop solutions often contain strong acids.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Yellow color in wells, indicating a successfully stopped enzymatic reaction.
troubleshooting: If blue color persists: Ensure sufficient stop solution volume or check its concentration.
If uneven color change: Ensure thorough mixing after adding stop solution.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Within 30 minutes of adding the stop solution, read the absorbance of each well at the primary wavelength specified by the ELISA kit (e.g., 450 nm) using a microplate reader. If a correction wavelength is recommended (e.g., 620 nm), read at both wavelengths and subtract the correction wavelength readings from the primary wavelength readings.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the exact primary and correction wavelengths (if any) from the chosen high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit instructions.
steps.notes: Timeliness is important; read the plate within the recommended window to minimize further color changes. Ensure the plate reader is properly calibrated.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Absorbance values for all wells, which will be used to construct the standard curve and calculate sample CRP concentrations.
troubleshooting: If readings are unstable: Ensure plate reader is clean and calibrated.
If values are outside linear range: Adjust pipetting volumes or dilute/concentrate samples in a subsequent run.

steps.title: Analyze Data
steps.body_md: Use appropriate software (e.g., spreadsheet program) to generate a standard curve by plotting the absorbance values (Y-axis) against the known CRP concentrations of the standards (X-axis). Apply a 4-parameter logistic (4PL) or 5-parameter logistic (5PL) curve fit as recommended by the kit manufacturer. Extrapolate the CRP concentrations of the spiked whole blood samples from their measured absorbance values using the standard curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet software or ELISA analysis software
todo_for_researcher: Identify and use the specific curve fitting model recommended by the ELISA kit manufacturer or standard laboratory practice for high-sensitivity assays.
steps.notes: Verify the R-squared value of the standard curve (typically >0.99) to ensure a good fit. Pay attention to the lower detection limits defined by the kit.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Calculated CRP concentrations for all spiked whole blood samples, and a limit of detection (LOD) for the assay.
troubleshooting: If standard curve R-squared is low: Recheck standard dilutions and pipetting accuracy, consider re-running the assay.
If sample readings are outside standard curve: Dilute or concentrate samples and re-run the assay.

equipment: Microcentrifuge tubes or appropriate dilution tubes
Computer with spreadsheet software or ELISA analysis software
reagents: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit (all components: microplate, standards, diluent, detection antibody, wash buffer, TMB substrate, stop solution)
Spiked whole blood samples (concentrations from 0.1-10 mg/L)

success_criteria.how_measured: R-squared value of the 4PL or 5PL curve fit
Absorbance of the zero standard (blank) wells
success_criteria.threshold: < 0.100 OD units at 450 nm (or as per kit specifications)

success_criteria.how_measured: Coefficient of Variation (CV) for absorbance values of duplicate/triplicate wells
Expected vs. measured CRP concentrations in spiked whole blood samples across the relevant range
success_criteria.threshold: Measured values should be within +/- 20% of expected spiked concentrations

procedures.name: Data Analysis and Performance Evaluation
procedures.intent: To calculate detection sensitivity (LOD) and compare time-to-result for both the biosensor and ELISA, establishing the biosensor's performance relative to the hypothesis.

steps.title: Collect and Organize Raw Data
steps.body_md: Gather all raw data from both the paper-based electrochemical biosensor measurements and the laboratory ELISA measurements. This includes, but is not limited to, electrochemical signals (e.g., current, voltage) for each CRP concentration, ELISA absorbance values, corresponding CRP concentrations for each spiked sample, and timestamps for each measurement.
equipment_needed: Laboratory notebook or electronic data management system
steps.notes: Ensure all data is clearly labeled and traceable to the specific samples and measurement conditions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A complete, organized dataset for both biosensor and ELISA measurements.

steps.title: Perform Biosensor Signal-Concentration Curve Fitting
steps.body_md: For the paper-based electrochemical biosensor data, plot the measured electrochemical signal (e.g., current) against the known spiked CRP concentrations. Fit an appropriate calibration curve (e.g., linear, sigmoidal) to the data points. Calculate the slope of the linear portion of the curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with statistical software (e.g., R, Python with SciPy/NumPy, GraphPad Prism)
todo_for_researcher: Determine the most appropriate fitting model for the biosensor data.
steps.notes: The slope of the calibration curve is crucial for LOD calculation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A fitted calibration curve and determined slope value for the biosensor data.

steps.title: Calculate Biosensor Limit of Detection (LOD)
steps.body_md: Calculate the Limit of Detection (LOD) for the paper-based biosensor using the formula: LOD = (3 * SD_blank) / slope, where SD_blank is the standard deviation of the electrochemical signal measurements from blank (0 mg/L CRP) whole blood samples, and 'slope' is the sensitivity derived from the signal-concentration curve fitting in the previous step.
steps.notes: Ensure a sufficient number of blank measurements for robust SD_blank calculation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A quantitative LOD value (in mg/L) for the paper-based biosensor.
troubleshooting: If LOD is unusually high: Recheck blank measurements for contamination or signal drift; verify slope calculation.

steps.title: Perform ELISA Signal-Concentration Curve Fitting
steps.body_md: For the laboratory ELISA data, plot the measured absorbance values against the known spiked CRP concentrations. Fit an appropriate calibration curve (e.g., 4-parameter logistic, linear) to the data points. Calculate the slope of the linear portion of the curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with statistical software (e.g., R, Python with SciPy/NumPy, GraphPad Prism)
todo_for_researcher: Determine the most appropriate fitting model for the ELISA data.
steps.notes: ELISA typically uses a non-linear fit, often a 4-parameter logistic model. For LOD, the slope in the low concentration range is relevant.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A fitted calibration curve and determined slope value for the ELISA data.

steps.title: Calculate ELISA Limit of Detection (LOD)
steps.body_md: Calculate the Limit of Detection (LOD) for the laboratory ELISA using the formula: LOD = (3 * SD_blank) / slope, where SD_blank is the standard deviation of the absorbance measurements from blank (0 mg/L CRP) whole blood samples, and 'slope' is the sensitivity derived from the signal-concentration curve fitting in the previous step (specifically, the slope in the low concentration range).
steps.notes: Similar to the biosensor, ensure robust blank measurements for accurate SD_blank.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A quantitative LOD value (in mg/L) for the ELISA.

steps.title: Compare LODs
steps.body_md: Compare the calculated LODs of the paper-based biosensor and the laboratory ELISA. Note whether the biosensor achieves an LOD below 0.5 mg/L.
steps.notes: This is a direct comparison against the hypothesis's sensitivity target.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A clear statement comparing the biosensor's LOD to the ELISA's LOD and the 0.5 mg/L threshold.

steps.title: Analyze Time-to-Result for Biosensor
steps.body_md: Review the recorded timestamps for the paper-based biosensor measurements. Determine the typical time taken from sample application to final readout for each CRP concentration tested. Calculate the average and standard deviation of these time-to-result values.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
steps.notes: This analysis should reflect the total time a user would spend to get a result.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Average and standard deviation of biosensor time-to-result.

steps.title: Analyze Time-to-Result for ELISA
steps.body_md: Review the recorded timestamps and protocol steps for the laboratory ELISA. Determine the total time required from sample setup (after any preceding sample preparation) to final readout for the ELISA. This should include incubation times, wash steps, and reading time.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
steps.notes: Ensure sample preparation time for ELISA is considered in the overall comparison if it significantly differs from biosensor's no-prep approach.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Estimated total time-to-result for the laboratory ELISA.

steps.title: Compare Time-to-Result
steps.body_md: Compare the average time-to-result for the paper-based biosensor with that of the laboratory ELISA. Note whether the biosensor achieves a result within 10 minutes.
steps.notes: This is a direct comparison against the hypothesis's time target.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A clear statement comparing the biosensor's time-to-result to the ELISA's and the 10-minute threshold.

steps.title: Conduct Statistical Comparisons of Sensitivity and Accuracy
steps.body_md: Perform appropriate statistical tests to formally compare the performance of the biosensor versus the ELISA. This may include t-tests or ANOVA to compare LODs if multiple replicates of blank measurements and calibration curves were performed. Additionally, assess the accuracy of the biosensor by comparing its quantitative readout to the known spiked concentrations, and potentially compare these to the ELISA's accuracy across the relevant concentration range.
equipment_needed: Computer with statistical software (e.g., R, Python, GraphPad Prism)
steps.notes: Consider Pearson correlation or Bland-Altman analysis for method comparison if a larger set of samples is available.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Statistical significance values and confidence intervals for comparisons of LOD and accuracy.
troubleshooting: If statistical assumptions are violated: Explore non-parametric tests or data transformations.
If results are inconclusive: Consider increasing replicates or sample size in future experiments.

steps.title: Summarize Findings and Draw Conclusions
steps.body_md: Consolidate all analyses regarding LOD, time-to-result, and statistical comparisons. Formulate conclusions addressing the research question: whether the paper-based electrochemical biosensor can detect CRP in whole blood at clinically relevant concentrations within 10 minutes, without sample preprocessing, relative to the performance of a laboratory ELISA.
steps.notes: This step should directly answer the hypothesis and research question.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A comprehensive summary of results, including tables, graphs, and a conclusion statement.

equipment: Laboratory notebook or electronic data management system
Statistical software (e.g., R, Python with SciPy/NumPy, GraphPad Prism)
Spreadsheet software (e.g., Microsoft Excel, Google Sheets)

success_criteria.how_measured: Calculated LOD from signal-concentration curve and blank standard deviation
Average time from sample addition to final signal readout
Statistical comparison of biosensor LOD vs. laboratory ELISA LOD
success_criteria.threshold: Not significantly worse than ELISA LOD (e.g., p > 0.05)

success_criteria.what: Accuracy of biosensor across clinically relevant range
success_criteria.how_measured: Comparison of biosensor readings to known spiked CRP concentrations (0.1-10 mg/L)
success_criteria.threshold: Mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) < 20% for concentrations > LOD

steps.title: Obtain and Prepare Whole Blood Sample
steps.body_md: Obtain fresh whole blood samples (EDTA or citrate anti-coagulated) from healthy donors. Ensure the blood is at room temperature (20-25°C) before proceeding. Gently invert the blood collection tube 5-10 times to ensure homogeneity, but avoid vigorous shaking that could induce hemolysis.
steps.notes: Using fresh blood is crucial for accurate CRP measurements as CRP levels can change over time in stored samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogenous whole blood sample, no visible clots or hemolysis.
troubleshooting: If visible clots are present: Do not use the sample; obtain a fresh one. Clots can interfere with accurate spiking and biosensor performance.
If heavy hemolysis is observed: Do not use the sample; obtain a fresh one. Hemolysis can artificially elevate CRP readings and interfere with optical detection.

steps.title: Prepare Concentrated CRP Stock Solution
steps.body_md: Carefully weigh out the required mass of lyophilized Human C-Reactive Protein (CRP) to prepare a concentrated stock solution. Dissolve the CRP in 1X PBS (phosphate-buffered saline, pH 7.4) to achieve an initial stock concentration of 100 mg/L. Mix gently by inversion until fully dissolved. For example, to make 1 mL of 100 mg/L stock, weigh 0.1 mg of CRP and dissolve in 1 mL PBS.
steps.notes: Ensure sterile conditions to avoid contamination, as these solutions will be introduced into blood samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, homogenous CRP stock solution.
troubleshooting: If CRP does not dissolve completely: Gently warm to 37°C for 5 minutes and vortex lightly, or extend dissolution time. Avoid vigorous vortexing that can denature the protein.

steps.title: Prepare Intermediate CRP Dilution Series
steps.body_md: From the 100 mg/L CRP stock solution, prepare a series of intermediate CRP dilutions in 1X PBS to minimize pipetting errors when spiking into whole blood. Prepare dilutions at 5 mg/L, 1 mg/L, and 0.2 mg/L. For example, to get 1 mL of 5 mg/L solution, take 50 μL of 100 mg/L stock and add 950 μL of PBS.
steps.notes: These intermediate dilutions should be freshly prepared right before spiking to ensure accuracy.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clear, accurately diluted CRP solutions.

steps.title: Aliquot Whole Blood for Spiking
steps.body_md: Aliquot specific volumes (e.g., 500 μL) of the homogenous whole blood sample into individual microcentrifuge tubes or appropriate assay tubes for each CRP concentration and for an unspiked control. Ensure accurate and consistent aliquoting.
steps.notes: Use separate tubes for each concentration to avoid cross-contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Consistent aliquots of whole blood in individual tubes.

steps.title: Spike Whole Blood with CRP
steps.body_md: Carefully add appropriate small volumes of the prepared CRP intermediate dilutions or stock solution into the aliquoted whole blood to achieve final target CRP concentrations of 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, and 10 mg/L. The spike volume should be ≤ 5% of the total blood volume to minimize dilution effects on the whole blood matrix. For example, to achieve a final concentration of 1 mg/L in 500 μL of blood, add 25 μL of a 20 mg/L CRP solution (which you would prepare from the 100 mg/L stock or by diluting the 5 mg/L solution further). Ensure gentle mixing immediately after spiking by inverting the tube 2-3 times.
todo_for_researcher: Calculate precise spike volumes and prepare specific intermediate dilutions to achieve target final concentrations within the 5% spike volume constraint. Consider preparing an additional intermediate CRP solution, e.g., 20 mg/L, if multiple target concentrations require it to stay within the 5% volumetric spike. For example, to reach 0.1 mg/L, using 5% spike, one would need to spike a 2 mg/L solution. To reach 0.5 mg/L, one would need to spike a 10 mg/L solution etc.
steps.notes: Accurate pipetting of the small spike volumes is critical. If pipetting very small volumes (e.g., <5 μL) directly, consider preparing more dilute intermediate CRP solutions to increase the spike volume and improve accuracy.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Whole blood samples spiked with a gradient of known CRP concentrations.
troubleshooting: If results are not reproducible across replicates: Re-check pipetting technique and calibration. Ensure thorough but gentle mixing after each spike.

steps.title: Incubate Spiked Samples
steps.body_md: Incubate the spiked whole blood samples at room temperature (20-25°C) for a short duration (10-15 minutes) to allow for complete CRP dissolution and equilibrium within the blood matrix. Do not incubate for extended periods to avoid potential changes in blood components or CRP stability.
steps.notes: Maintain consistent temperature across all samples.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Spiked samples ready for immediate analysis, with CRP uniformly distributed.

steps.title: Prepare Paper Substrate
steps.body_md: Cut chromatography paper (e.g., Whatman Grade 1 CHR) into desired biosensor dimensions (e.g., 1.5 cm x 4 cm rectangles) using a precision cutter. Ensure cuts are clean and free of tearing or fraying.
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact dimensions (length and width) if not 1.5 cm x 4 cm.
steps.notes: The choice of paper impacts capillary flow and mechanical stability. Whatman Grade 1 CHR is a common choice for its uniformity.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Uniformly cut paper rectangles ready for patterning.

steps.title: Pattern Hydrophobic Barriers
steps.body_md: Apply a hydrophobic material (e.g., SU-8 photoresist using photolithography, or wax using a wax printer) to define the electrochemical sensing zones and fluidic channels on the paper substrate. Cure or set the hydrophobic material according to the manufacturer's instructions. Ensure clear, well-defined boundaries to prevent sample wicking outside the desired areas.
equipment_needed: Photolithography equipment (for SU-8) or wax printer (for wax)
Oven or hot plate (if curing is required)
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact hydrophobic patterning method (e.g., wax printing, screen printing, photolithography).
Specify the exact dimensions and layout of the hydrophobic channels/sensing area.
steps.notes: This step is critical for directing fluid flow and electrically isolating different regions of the sensor.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Paper substrates with clearly defined hydrophobic barriers and hydrophilic channels/sensing areas.
troubleshooting: If hydrophobic barriers are indistinct: Re-evaluate pattern design or material application parameters (e.g., wax temperature, SU-8 exposure time).
If sample wicks outside channels: Increase barrier thickness or adjust material properties.

steps.title: Deposit Electrochemical Electrodes
steps.body_md: Deposit conductive material (e.g., carbon ink, silver/silver chloride ink) onto the defined sensing zones using screen printing or stencil printing. Create a three-electrode system: working electrode, counter electrode, and reference electrode. Cure the conductive ink as per manufacturer's instructions (e.g., oven baking).
todo_for_researcher: Specify exact ink formulations and deposition methods.
Specify electrode dimensions and spacing based on the patterned design.
steps.notes: Careful deposition ensures consistent electrical conductivity and electrochemical performance.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Paper substrates with clearly visible and conductive electrochemical electrodes.
troubleshooting: If electrodes are not conductive: Check printing parameters, ink viscosity, and curing conditions.
If electrodes are smeared: Optimize printing pressure or stencil/screen alignment.

steps.title: Block Non-Specific Binding Sites
steps.body_md: Incubate the fabricated biosensors with a blocking solution (e.g., 1% Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) in Phosphate Buffered Saline (PBS) or 0.5% Casein in PBS) for 1 hour at room temperature. This step reduces non-specific binding of assay components and analytes to the biosensor surface.
todo_for_researcher: Specify the exact blocking agent and its concentration if not 1% BSA or 0.5% Casein.
steps.notes: Adequate blocking is essential for achieving a low background signal and high specificity.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Biosensors visibly wet with blocking solution.
troubleshooting: If high background signal in later steps: Increase blocking agent concentration or incubation time.

steps.title: Wash After Blocking
steps.body_md: Gently rinse the biosensors three times with PBS (pH 7.4) for 5 minutes each wash. Ensure complete removal of unbound blocking solution. Blot excess liquid on a lint-free tissue.
steps.notes: Thorough washing prevents interference with subsequent functionalization steps.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clean biosensor surfaces without visible residue.

steps.title: Immobilize Anti-CRP Antibodies
steps.body_md: Apply 5 µL of anti-CRP antibody solution (100 µg/mL in PBS, pH 7.4) onto the working electrode surface of each biosensor. Incubate in a humid chamber for 2 hours at room temperature to allow for antibody immobilization. Protect from light.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm optimal anti-CRP antibody concentration (100 µg/mL is a starting point, may require optimization).
Specify the exact immobilization chemistry if specific covalent binding is used (e.g., EDC/NHS coupling).
steps.notes: Antibody concentration and immobilization time are critical for achieving high sensitivity and reducing variability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Antibody solution absorbed into the working electrode area.
troubleshooting: If low signal in later steps: Increase antibody concentration or incubation time.
If uneven antibody distribution: Ensure electrode surface is fully hydrated before application.

steps.title: Wash After Antibody Immobilization
steps.body_md: Gently rinse the biosensors three times with PBS containing 0.05% Tween 20 (PBST) for 5 minutes each wash to remove unbound antibodies. Blot excess liquid on a lint-free tissue.
steps.notes: Adequate washing minimizes background noise by removing non-specifically adsorbed antibodies.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clean biosensor surfaces without visible antibody residue.

steps.title: Dry Fabricated Biosensors
steps.body_md: Allow the functionalized biosensors to air dry at room temperature (20-25°C) and controlled humidity (e.g., 40-50% RH) for at least 4 hours, or until completely dry. This can be done in a desiccator or a controlled environment chamber.
steps.notes: Proper drying is crucial for long-term stability and consistent performance. Avoid rapid drying, which can denature antibodies.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Completely dry biosensors, ready for packaging or immediate use.
troubleshooting: If biosensors remain damp: Extend drying time or ensure proper humidity control.
If antibody denatures (loss of function): Reduce drying temperature or consider lyophilization if long-term storage is needed.

steps.title: Package and Store Biosensors
steps.body_md: Package the dried biosensors individually in sealed, opaque, airtight bags containing desiccant packets. Store at 4°C until use. Avoid exposure to light and excessive humidity.
steps.notes: Proper storage extends the shelf life of the functionalized biosensors and maintains antibody stability.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Packaged, stable biosensors ready for future experiments.
troubleshooting: If biosensor performance degrades over time: Check storage conditions (temperature, humidity, light exposure) and desiccant efficacy.

steps.title: Prepare room temperature environment
steps.body_md: Ensure the laboratory environment is maintained at room temperature (20-25°C). This is crucial for consistent electrochemical measurements and enzymatic reaction kinetics.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Working environment is within the specified temperature range.
troubleshooting: If temperature is outside range: Adjust HVAC or relocate to a controlled environment.

steps.title: Calibrate electrochemical workstation
steps.body_md: Perform a self-calibration and baseline noise check of the potentiostat/galvanostat according to the manufacturer's instructions. Ensure electrodes are clean and connections are secure.
steps.notes: Calibration ensures accurate and reproducible electrochemical readings.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Electrochemical workstation passes self-calibration and displays stable baseline current.
troubleshooting: If calibration fails: Check electrode connections, clean electrodes if necessary, and restart the instrument. Consult manual for error codes.
If high noise is observed: Check for electromagnetic interference, ensure proper grounding.

steps.title: Prepare spiked whole blood samples
steps.body_md: Aliquot 1 mL of each pre-spiked whole blood sample (CRP concentrations spanning 0.1-10 mg/L) into sterile microcentrifuge tubes. Ensure samples are gently inverted 5 times to ensure homogeneity immediately prior to use.
steps.notes: Whole blood can settle; mixing is essential for accurate aliquotting and consistent results.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Homogeneous 1 mL aliquots of spiked whole blood samples ready for application.

steps.title: Retrieve biosensors and connect to workstation
steps.body_md: Carefully remove a paper-based electrochemical biosensor from its packaging using clean forceps. Connect the biosensor's working, reference, and counter electrodes to the corresponding leads of the electrochemical workstation.
steps.notes: Handle biosensors by the edges to avoid contamination of the active detection area.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Biosensor securely connected to the workstation, ready for sample application.
troubleshooting: If connections are loose: Gently re-seat the electrode leads until secure. Loose connections lead to noisy or erroneous signals.

steps.title: Apply whole blood sample to biosensor
steps.body_md: Pipette a precise volume of 10 µL of the prepared whole blood sample onto the active sensing area of the paper-based biosensor. Immediately start a timer. Avoid introducing air bubbles during application.
todo_for_researcher: Determine the precise 'active sensing area' for sample application based on biosensor design.
steps.notes: Consistent sample volume and precise application location are critical for reproducibility. The timer tracks the incubation and detection time.
steps.anticipated_outcome: 10 µL blood sample evenly spread on the biosensor's active area.
troubleshooting: If air bubbles form: Carefully try to disperse them using the pipette tip without touching the sensing surface, or discard and re-apply with a new sensor if significant.

steps.title: Incubate sample on biosensor
steps.body_md: Allow the whole blood sample to incubate on the biosensor at room temperature for the specified duration (up to 10 minutes). The CRP in the sample will bind to the anti-CRP antibodies immobilized on the sensor surface.
steps.notes: This incubation period allows for sufficient binding kinetics. The specific duration for optimal signal-to-noise ratio within the 10-minute window will be explored.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Binding of CRP to the biosensor surface occurring.

steps.title: Perform electrochemical measurement (Amperometry or Voltammetry)
steps.body_md: At the desired time point (e.g., 5 min, 7 min, or 10 min from sample application), initiate the electrochemical measurement program on the workstation. If using amperometry, apply a constant potential at +0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl reference electrode and record the current for 60 seconds. If using voltammetry, perform a Square Wave Voltammetry (SWV) scan from -0.2 V to +0.4 V vs. Ag/AgCl.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the specific potential and parameters (frequency, amplitude) for amperometry/SWV based on preliminary biosensor characterization. These values are common starting points.
steps.notes: Begin data recording simultaneously with the potential application. The current or peak current will be proportional to the captured CRP concentration after the enzymatic reaction if a reporter enzyme system is used. This step assumes a completed enzymatic reporter step (e.g., alkaline phosphatase/p-aminophenyl phosphate or HRP/TMB) has been performed if needed for signal generation, which might be a preceding step in a full immunoassay. Given the context of a rapid biosensor, it's assumed the signal generation is intrinsic or very rapid.
steps.anticipated_outcome: An electrochemical current (amperometry) or peak current (voltammetry) signal is recorded by the workstation.
troubleshooting: If no signal or noisy signal: Recheck electrode connections, ensure the sample covers the active area, verify workstation settings.

steps.title: Record electrochemical data and time-to-result
steps.body_md: Save the recorded current-time (amperometry) or current-potential (voltammetry) data files. Note the exact time elapsed from sample application (Step 5) to the initiation of the electrochemical measurement (Step 7) as the 'time-to-result'.
steps.notes: Accurate time-to-result recording is a key parameter of this experiment.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Raw electrochemical data and corresponding time-to-result values are saved digitally.
troubleshooting: If data file corruption: Ensure enough disk space and proper software termination. Backup data regularly.

steps.title: Repeat for all samples and controls
steps.body_md: Repeat steps 4 through 8 for all prepared spiked whole blood samples covering the concentration range (0.1-10 mg/L) and any relevant controls (e.g., blank whole blood, unspiked whole blood). Use a fresh biosensor for each measurement.
steps.notes: Using a fresh biosensor for each sample prevents carry-over contamination and ensures independent measurements. A calibration curve will be generated from these measurements.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A complete dataset of electrochemical signals and time-to-result values for all CRP concentrations and controls.

steps.title: Wash biosensor (if applicable)
steps.body_md: If a wash step is integral to the biosensor design (e.g., to remove unbound matrix components), perform a gentle wash immediately following the incubation period (Step 6) and prior to electrochemical measurement (Step 7). Use 100 µL of PBS (pH 7.4) and allow it to wick across the sensor for 30 seconds. Repeat 2 times. Gently blot excess liquid with filter paper, avoiding the active sensing area.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm if a wash step is required for the specific biosensor design provided. If not, omit this step.
steps.notes: A wash step improves signal-to-noise ratio by removing non-specifically bound components and interfering substances from the whole blood matrix.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Reduction in background signal and improved specificity without impacting specifically bound CRP.
troubleshooting: If signal decreases unexpectedly: Excessive or harsh washing might remove bound analytes. Optimize wash volume and duration.
If background remains high: Insufficient washing, increase wash volume or number of repetitions.

steps.title: Prepare Standards and Samples
steps.body_md: Prepare a series of CRP standards using the diluent provided in the high-sensitivity ELISA kit. Create at least 6 standard concentrations spanning the expected linear range of CRP detection (e.g., 0.05 mg/L to 10 mg/L), ensuring at least two points cover the clinically relevant range below 0.5 mg/L.  Prepare the spiked whole blood samples according to the experimental plan (e.g., undiluted whole blood spiked with known CRP concentrations from 0.1-10 mg/L). Allow samples and standards to equilibrate to room temperature (20-25 °C) before use.
equipment_needed: Microcentrifuge tubes or appropriate dilution tubes
steps.notes: Ensure all pipettes are calibrated to ensure accurate dilution of standards.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A complete set of standards and spiked whole blood samples ready for plating.
troubleshooting: If standard curve concentrations are inaccurate: Recalibrate pipettes and redo dilutions.
If reagents are not at room temperature: Allow more time for equilibration to avoid temperature-dependent assay variations.

steps.title: Add Standards and Samples to Microplate
steps.body_md: Remove the microplate from its sealed pouch and allow it to equilibrate to room temperature (20-25 °C). Pipette 50 µL of each standard, control, and spiked whole blood sample into appropriate wells of the ELISA microplate in duplicate or triplicate, as recommended by the kit instructions. Ensure a plate map is meticulously documented.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the exact recommended well volume from the chosen ELISA kit instructions.
steps.notes: Avoid splashing contents between wells. Change pipette tips meticulously between each standard/sample to prevent cross-contamination.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Standards, controls, and samples accurately dispensed into the microplate wells.
troubleshooting: If cross-contamination is suspected: Discard contaminated wells/plate and restart.
If bubbles are present in wells: Gently tap the plate or use a fresh pipette tip to remove them, being careful not to remove liquid.

steps.title: Add Detection Antibody and Incubate
steps.body_md: Add 50 µL of the high-sensitivity CRP detection antibody (conjugate or secondary antibody, as supplied by the kit) to each well containing standards, controls, and samples. Gently tap the plate to ensure thorough mixing. Cover the plate with an adhesive plate seal or lid to prevent evaporation and incubate at room temperature (20-25 °C) for a duration specified in the kit instructions (e.g., 60 minutes).
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit detection antibody
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact incubation time for the chosen high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit.
Confirm if the detection antibody needs prior dilution according to kit instructions.
steps.notes: Ensure even distribution of the detection antibody. Incubation time and temperature are critical for antibody binding kinetics.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Antibodies binding to CRP antigens in the wells.
troubleshooting: If inconsistent signal across plate: Ensure proper mixing and consistent incubation conditions across all wells.
If evaporation: Ensure plate is properly sealed during incubation.

steps.title: Wash Microplate
steps.body_md: Carefully remove the plate seal. Discard the liquid from the wells by inverting the plate over a waste container. Wash each well extensively 3-5 times with 250-300 µL of 1X wash buffer, ensuring no residual liquid remains after each wash by sharply tapping the inverted plate on absorbent paper. Ensure proper aspiration if using an automated plate washer.
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit 1X wash buffer
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the precise number of washes and wash buffer volume specified in the chosen ELISA kit instructions.
steps.notes: Thorough washing is crucial to remove unbound antibody and reduce background signal. Incomplete aspiration leads to high background.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Clean wells with minimal background signal, ready for substrate addition.
troubleshooting: If high background signal after washing: Increase number of washes or ensure complete removal of wash buffer.
If wells appear dry or scratched: Adjust plate washer settings or ensure gentle manual washing.

steps.title: Add Substrate and Incubate
steps.body_md: Add 100 µL of the TMB (3,3',5,5'-Tetramethylbenzidine) substrate solution to each well. Gently tap the plate to ensure mixing. Incubate the plate at room temperature (20-25 °C) in the dark for the duration specified in the kit instructions (e.g., 15-30 minutes). Color development will occur during this step, transitioning from clear to blue.
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit TMB substrate solution
todo_for_researcher: Determine the exact substrate incubation time for the chosen high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit.
steps.notes: Protect the substrate from light exposure during incubation as it is light-sensitive. Monitor color development, especially for higher concentration standards.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Color development (blue) in wells, proportional to CRP concentration.
troubleshooting: If no color development: Check integrity of substrate solution; ensure proper addition of all reagents.
If color develops too quickly/slowly: Adjust incubation time or check temperature consistency.

steps.title: Add Stop Solution
steps.body_md: Add 50 µL of the stop solution (e.g., sulfuric acid) to each well. Gently tap the plate to ensure thorough mixing. The blue color developed in the previous step will immediately change to yellow, indicating the reaction has been stopped.
reagents_referenced: High-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit stop solution
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the exact volume of stop solution required by the chosen ELISA kit.
steps.notes: Add stop solution in the same order and at a similar rate as the substrate to ensure consistent reaction times. Wear appropriate personal protective equipment (PPE) as stop solutions often contain strong acids.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Yellow color in wells, indicating a successfully stopped enzymatic reaction.
troubleshooting: If blue color persists: Ensure sufficient stop solution volume or check its concentration.
If uneven color change: Ensure thorough mixing after adding stop solution.

steps.title: Read Absorbance
steps.body_md: Within 30 minutes of adding the stop solution, read the absorbance of each well at the primary wavelength specified by the ELISA kit (e.g., 450 nm) using a microplate reader. If a correction wavelength is recommended (e.g., 620 nm), read at both wavelengths and subtract the correction wavelength readings from the primary wavelength readings.
todo_for_researcher: Confirm the exact primary and correction wavelengths (if any) from the chosen high-sensitivity CRP ELISA kit instructions.
steps.notes: Timeliness is important; read the plate within the recommended window to minimize further color changes. Ensure the plate reader is properly calibrated.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Absorbance values for all wells, which will be used to construct the standard curve and calculate sample CRP concentrations.
troubleshooting: If readings are unstable: Ensure plate reader is clean and calibrated.
If values are outside linear range: Adjust pipetting volumes or dilute/concentrate samples in a subsequent run.

steps.title: Analyze Data
steps.body_md: Use appropriate software (e.g., spreadsheet program) to generate a standard curve by plotting the absorbance values (Y-axis) against the known CRP concentrations of the standards (X-axis). Apply a 4-parameter logistic (4PL) or 5-parameter logistic (5PL) curve fit as recommended by the kit manufacturer. Extrapolate the CRP concentrations of the spiked whole blood samples from their measured absorbance values using the standard curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet software or ELISA analysis software
todo_for_researcher: Identify and use the specific curve fitting model recommended by the ELISA kit manufacturer or standard laboratory practice for high-sensitivity assays.
steps.notes: Verify the R-squared value of the standard curve (typically >0.99) to ensure a good fit. Pay attention to the lower detection limits defined by the kit.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Calculated CRP concentrations for all spiked whole blood samples, and a limit of detection (LOD) for the assay.
troubleshooting: If standard curve R-squared is low: Recheck standard dilutions and pipetting accuracy, consider re-running the assay.
If sample readings are outside standard curve: Dilute or concentrate samples and re-run the assay.

steps.title: Collect and Organize Raw Data
steps.body_md: Gather all raw data from both the paper-based electrochemical biosensor measurements and the laboratory ELISA measurements. This includes, but is not limited to, electrochemical signals (e.g., current, voltage) for each CRP concentration, ELISA absorbance values, corresponding CRP concentrations for each spiked sample, and timestamps for each measurement.
equipment_needed: Laboratory notebook or electronic data management system
steps.notes: Ensure all data is clearly labeled and traceable to the specific samples and measurement conditions.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A complete, organized dataset for both biosensor and ELISA measurements.

steps.title: Perform Biosensor Signal-Concentration Curve Fitting
steps.body_md: For the paper-based electrochemical biosensor data, plot the measured electrochemical signal (e.g., current) against the known spiked CRP concentrations. Fit an appropriate calibration curve (e.g., linear, sigmoidal) to the data points. Calculate the slope of the linear portion of the curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with statistical software (e.g., R, Python with SciPy/NumPy, GraphPad Prism)
todo_for_researcher: Determine the most appropriate fitting model for the biosensor data.
steps.notes: The slope of the calibration curve is crucial for LOD calculation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A fitted calibration curve and determined slope value for the biosensor data.

steps.title: Calculate Biosensor Limit of Detection (LOD)
steps.body_md: Calculate the Limit of Detection (LOD) for the paper-based biosensor using the formula: LOD = (3 * SD_blank) / slope, where SD_blank is the standard deviation of the electrochemical signal measurements from blank (0 mg/L CRP) whole blood samples, and 'slope' is the sensitivity derived from the signal-concentration curve fitting in the previous step.
steps.notes: Ensure a sufficient number of blank measurements for robust SD_blank calculation.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A quantitative LOD value (in mg/L) for the paper-based biosensor.
troubleshooting: If LOD is unusually high: Recheck blank measurements for contamination or signal drift; verify slope calculation.

steps.title: Perform ELISA Signal-Concentration Curve Fitting
steps.body_md: For the laboratory ELISA data, plot the measured absorbance values against the known spiked CRP concentrations. Fit an appropriate calibration curve (e.g., 4-parameter logistic, linear) to the data points. Calculate the slope of the linear portion of the curve.
equipment_needed: Computer with statistical software (e.g., R, Python with SciPy/NumPy, GraphPad Prism)
todo_for_researcher: Determine the most appropriate fitting model for the ELISA data.
steps.notes: ELISA typically uses a non-linear fit, often a 4-parameter logistic model. For LOD, the slope in the low concentration range is relevant.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A fitted calibration curve and determined slope value for the ELISA data.

steps.title: Calculate ELISA Limit of Detection (LOD)
steps.body_md: Calculate the Limit of Detection (LOD) for the laboratory ELISA using the formula: LOD = (3 * SD_blank) / slope, where SD_blank is the standard deviation of the absorbance measurements from blank (0 mg/L CRP) whole blood samples, and 'slope' is the sensitivity derived from the signal-concentration curve fitting in the previous step (specifically, the slope in the low concentration range).
steps.notes: Similar to the biosensor, ensure robust blank measurements for accurate SD_blank.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A quantitative LOD value (in mg/L) for the ELISA.

steps.title: Compare LODs
steps.body_md: Compare the calculated LODs of the paper-based biosensor and the laboratory ELISA. Note whether the biosensor achieves an LOD below 0.5 mg/L.
steps.notes: This is a direct comparison against the hypothesis's sensitivity target.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A clear statement comparing the biosensor's LOD to the ELISA's LOD and the 0.5 mg/L threshold.

steps.title: Analyze Time-to-Result for Biosensor
steps.body_md: Review the recorded timestamps for the paper-based biosensor measurements. Determine the typical time taken from sample application to final readout for each CRP concentration tested. Calculate the average and standard deviation of these time-to-result values.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
steps.notes: This analysis should reflect the total time a user would spend to get a result.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Average and standard deviation of biosensor time-to-result.

steps.title: Analyze Time-to-Result for ELISA
steps.body_md: Review the recorded timestamps and protocol steps for the laboratory ELISA. Determine the total time required from sample setup (after any preceding sample preparation) to final readout for the ELISA. This should include incubation times, wash steps, and reading time.
equipment_needed: Computer with spreadsheet or statistical software
steps.notes: Ensure sample preparation time for ELISA is considered in the overall comparison if it significantly differs from biosensor's no-prep approach.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Estimated total time-to-result for the laboratory ELISA.

steps.title: Compare Time-to-Result
steps.body_md: Compare the average time-to-result for the paper-based biosensor with that of the laboratory ELISA. Note whether the biosensor achieves a result within 10 minutes.
steps.notes: This is a direct comparison against the hypothesis's time target.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A clear statement comparing the biosensor's time-to-result to the ELISA's and the 10-minute threshold.

steps.title: Conduct Statistical Comparisons of Sensitivity and Accuracy
steps.body_md: Perform appropriate statistical tests to formally compare the performance of the biosensor versus the ELISA. This may include t-tests or ANOVA to compare LODs if multiple replicates of blank measurements and calibration curves were performed. Additionally, assess the accuracy of the biosensor by comparing its quantitative readout to the known spiked concentrations, and potentially compare these to the ELISA's accuracy across the relevant concentration range.
equipment_needed: Computer with statistical software (e.g., R, Python, GraphPad Prism)
steps.notes: Consider Pearson correlation or Bland-Altman analysis for method comparison if a larger set of samples is available.
steps.anticipated_outcome: Statistical significance values and confidence intervals for comparisons of LOD and accuracy.
troubleshooting: If statistical assumptions are violated: Explore non-parametric tests or data transformations.
If results are inconclusive: Consider increasing replicates or sample size in future experiments.

steps.title: Summarize Findings and Draw Conclusions
steps.body_md: Consolidate all analyses regarding LOD, time-to-result, and statistical comparisons. Formulate conclusions addressing the research question: whether the paper-based electrochemical biosensor can detect CRP in whole blood at clinically relevant concentrations within 10 minutes, without sample preprocessing, relative to the performance of a laboratory ELISA.
steps.notes: This step should directly answer the hypothesis and research question.
steps.anticipated_outcome: A comprehensive summary of results, including tables, graphs, and a conclusion statement.

Researchers are trained in handling whole blood samples and infectious materials.
Biosafety Level 2 (BSL-2) practices will be followed for handling whole blood.
Commercial ELISA kits are reliable and perform to manufacturer specifications.
Ethical approval for human blood sample collection and use is obtained.
